shortDefinition: Fasting insulin measures circulating insulin after an overnight fast and is one of the earliest signals of insulin resistance — often abnormal years before glucose.
fullDefinition:
## What fasting insulin measures

Fasting insulin (also called an **insulin level**) is a blood test that measures the amount of insulin in your bloodstream after at least 8 to 12 hours without food. Insulin is the hormone your pancreas releases to move glucose out of the blood and into cells. When the body becomes resistant to insulin's signal, the pancreas compensates by pumping out more — and that elevation shows up on a fasting insulin test long before fasting glucose or [hba1c] move out of range.

At Modern Thyroid Clinic, fasting insulin is a core part of how we evaluate metabolic health in women with thyroid and hormone symptoms, because [insulin-resistance] drives weight gain, fatigue, hormone imbalance, and inflammation that often masquerade as thyroid problems.

## Why it matters

Insulin is far more than a blood-sugar hormone. Chronically elevated insulin:

- Drives fat storage and makes weight loss extremely difficult
- Disrupts ovulation and worsens [polycystic-ovary-syndrome]
- Lowers sex hormone binding globulin (SHBG), raising free androgens
- Suppresses the conversion of T4 to active T3
- Fuels systemic inflammation and cardiovascular risk

Because fasting glucose can stay normal for a decade while insulin quietly climbs, checking insulin alone catches metabolic dysfunction much earlier than the standard panel most women receive.

## Reference range vs. functional/optimal range

**Conventional reference range:** typically less than 25 mIU/L (some labs use <19 or <12). The conventional range is wide because it is built around the population, much of which is already metabolically unhealthy.

**Functional/optimal target:** less than 7 mIU/L, with many functional clinicians aiming for 2–5 mIU/L in healthy, metabolically flexible women.

Fasting insulin is most useful when paired with fasting glucose to calculate **HOMA-IR**, a quick estimate of insulin resistance.

## What abnormal results suggest

**Elevated fasting insulin** (>7 mIU/L) suggests insulin resistance, often driven by excess refined carbohydrate intake, chronic stress, sleep deprivation, sedentary behavior, perimenopausal hormone shifts, or PCOS. It is also strongly associated with [prediabetes], [metabolic-syndrome], and increased risk of breast cancer and dementia.

**Very low fasting insulin** is rarely a problem but can be seen in advanced type 1 diabetes or pancreatic insufficiency.

The encouraging news: fasting insulin is one of the most responsive labs in functional medicine. Targeted nutrition, strength training, sleep, and treating underlying thyroid or hormone imbalance can lower it meaningfully within weeks. If your fasting insulin is elevated — or has never been checked — that is a conversation worth having with your clinician.

commonQuestions.q: Why isn't fasting insulin part of a standard checkup?
commonQuestions.a: Most primary care panels were built around diabetes diagnosis, which uses glucose and hba1c — not the upstream hormone that drives the disease. Insurance coverage for fasting insulin varies, and many physicians simply weren't trained to order it. The result is that millions of women have been told their labs are 'normal' for years while insulin resistance progresses silently. At Modern Thyroid Clinic we run fasting insulin on every metabolic workup precisely because it catches the problem a decade earlier than standard testing.

commonQuestions.q: How do I prepare for the test?
commonQuestions.a: Fast for 8 to 12 hours before the draw — water only, no coffee, tea, gum, or supplements. Schedule the lab for the morning, ideally before 10 a.m., when fasting hormones are most stable. Avoid intense exercise the night before, and don't change your eating pattern in the days leading up to the test (a 'cleanup week' will artificially lower the result and hide the real picture). If you take medications, ask your clinician whether any should be paused, but never stop prescribed medications without guidance.

commonQuestions.q: Can I lower my fasting insulin without medication?
commonQuestions.a: For most women, yes — and often substantially. The most effective levers are reducing refined carbohydrates and ultra-processed foods, building muscle through resistance training, walking after meals, prioritizing 7–9 hours of sleep, and managing stress. Adequate protein, fiber, and minerals like magnesium also help. When PCOS, thyroid disease, or perimenopause are driving the insulin resistance, treating the underlying condition is part of the answer. Medication is reserved for cases that don't respond to lifestyle work or where rapid intervention is needed.

seoTitle: Fasting Insulin Test: Optimal Range & What It Means | MTC
seoDescription: Fasting insulin catches insulin resistance years before glucose rises. Learn the optimal range, why it matters, and what abnormal results mean.
